title: Partnership in the Gospel
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Partnership in Gospel_ 2 Corinthians 1_21 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt

outline.point: I. The Danger of Lording It Over Ministry
subpoints: A. Paul defends himself against accusations by denying he dominates
B. True leadership is servant leadership modeled by Christ (Mark 10:45)
C. Peter and Paul both warn against controlling rather than serving

outline.point: II. Paul Instructs: Work With Others for Their Joy
subpoints: A. Paul's goal is not obedience or fear but the Corinthians' joy in Christ
B. Serving others for their joy—not ours—is the call of all believers
C. The bike-riding illustration: walking alongside produces joy and growth

outline.point: III. Standing Firm in Faith Through Partnership
subpoints: A. Faith is strengthened by partnership, not by isolation
B. Standing firm does not mean standing alone (Hebrews 10:23-25)
C. The word 'stir up' (Greek): an older brother poking a younger toward love
A. Church leaders: lead humbly, build faith and joy, not boss or master
B. All believers: walk alongside others, encourage, be mentored, share the gospel
C. Families: siblings are to encourage each other in Christ, point to the Lord
D. God’s work is done together in partnership, not alone

summary:
In this afternoon sermon from 2 Corinthians 1:23-24, Pastor Tito Lyro defends Paul's ministry against the accusation that he was domineering over the Corinthian church. Paul's key statement—'Not that we have dominion over your faith but are fellow workers for your joy'—becomes the text for a meditation on the nature of servant leadership. True spiritual growth and joy are produced when believers work together in faith, not when leaders dominate or when independence replaces partnership.

Using a boat-rowing analogy and a teaching-a-child-to-ride-a-bike illustration, Pastor Lyro shows how partnership produces far better results than control. The sermon applies broadly to church leaders, all members, and families: siblings are not merely recipients of spiritual care but active encouragers of one another in Christ. Ministry is partnership—walking alongside, pointing to Christ, and stirring one another up to love and good works together.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Boat Illustration

All right, this is going to involve some participation on your part. Has anybody rowed a boat? Raise your hand if you have rowed a boat.

Has any of you been in — not a charity boat — but like a little dinghy with two people, each one with a paddle? How successful are you when people are not paddling in harmony? What are a couple of things that might happen? You go in circles — roll, roll, roll your boat round about the stream instead of down the stream.

So imagine this scenario: two people trying to row a boat across a lake. One person grabs the oars and yells at the other, telling them exactly how to move. The other person sits silently, scared to make a mistake. How is that going to work for crossing the lake? How fast are you going to get to the other side? Not very fast — and perhaps not in a straight line either.

Now think of the same boat, but this time they communicate, they encourage one another, and each of the rowers does his or her part. How does that boat move? Much faster, much straighter — it will glide across the water to the other side.

This is what Paul is talking about — not about boats, but working together. Look at verse 24 of chapter one:

> *"Not that we have dominion over your faith, but are fellow workers for your joy; for by faith you stand."* (2 Corinthians 1:24)

## I. The Danger of Lording It Over Ministry

Paul is writing to the Corinthians to defend his ministry amid the accusations we have already seen. We have seen three accusations so far, and there are all kinds of other accusations that are going to come through as we go through this book. Some questioned his authority; others said he was too harsh. Yet he had poured himself out for them in love.

Paul says, "I am not here to control you. I am here to work with you so that your faith is strengthened and your joy is full."

It is a great temptation as a pastor. The pastor in some ways has no authority, but in a lot of ways has a lot of authority. Every time somebody stands up and opens the Word of God, there is a lot of authority that comes with that. And it is really tempting to try to control people — even though it is not possible — and to try to add burdens so that people go the same way. But ministry is not about control. Ministry is about partnership. Paul often saw himself as a partner of the church — not its boss, not its master, but a partner. True spiritual growth and joy are produced when we work together in faith, not when we are dominated and not when we dominate. That is not really how we grow in our faith.

This sermon is pretty simple this afternoon, like most afternoon sermons — at least I try to make them simple. Just three parts: I want us to see from this one verse that there is a danger in lording it over ministry; I want us to see that Paul instructs us to work with others for their joy; and lastly, I want us to see that in doing that we stand firm in the faith. If we partner rather than dominate, if we work together for other people's joy, we are going to grow in the faith.

Have we ever been around someone who felt the need to control everyone else? That is what Paul says he does not do. That is not how he carries himself in the ministry.

True leadership in God's kingdom is servant leadership. Jesus himself gave us the perfect example when he says in Mark 10:45:

> *"The Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life a ransom for many."* (Mark 10:45)

That is how he also taught his disciples in the parallel passage in Matthew 20:26:

> *"Yet it shall not be so among you; but whoever desires to become great among you, let him be your servant. And whoever desires to be first among you, let him be your slave."* (Matthew 20:26-27)

And this is in the context of leadership. He says the Gentiles lead by lording it over their people — but that is not how his followers are to do it.

Peter echoes this as well in 1 Peter 5:2-3:

> *"Shepherd the flock of God which is among you, serving as overseers, not by compulsion but willingly, not for dishonest gain but eagerly; nor as being lords over those entrusted to you, but being examples to the flock."* (1 Peter 5:2-3)

So leadership — and this is not just in the church, but leadership in general — is not about control. God calls us to lead by example, by humility, and by service. This is true of the husband as well: the husband is supposed to lead his wife — not to control, but to lead by example and partnership.

There is a danger in lording it over others. There is a danger that we will equate leadership with controlling people. When we do that, we forget that we are not their master — Christ is. We place ourselves in the position of Christ instead of pointing those under our leadership to Christ, who is the Lord, not us.

## II. Working With Others for Their Joy

Instead of lording over, we are to work with others for their joy. Look at verse 24 again:

> *"Not that we have dominion over your faith, but are fellow workers for your joy."* (2 Corinthians 1:24)

Paul contrasts control with collaboration. We work with you for your joy. If we were to get a concordance or Bible software and look up the word "joy," we would find that other people's joy is supposed to be a great motivator for service on our part — that we serve others because we want them to be joyful.

We are not called to directly increase our own joy. Our joy is filled indirectly as we die to self and serve others. The Bible does not teach that everything is about our own joy. We are supposed to be joy producers, not joy consumers. And notice that Paul's goal is not their obedience to him, not that they fear him — his goal is their joy.

This is a beautiful reminder: ministry is meant to produce joy in Christ — not guilt, not pressure, not fear — but joy in Christ.

Think about mentoring someone, or discipling someone, or even teaching a child. When you do not force them to learn, but walk with them, encourage them, cheer them on, celebrate their progress — that is when great growth takes place. That is really what Paul is doing here: he is building joy in Corinth, not walls.

Paul teaches this in other places as well. In Philippians 2:1-4, he tells us to look not only to our own interests, but also to the interests of others. In Galatians 6:2, he tells us to bear one another's burdens so that we can fulfill the law of Christ. In Romans 12:15, he teaches that we are to rejoice with those who rejoice — because joy is not selfish. Joy is relational. It builds partnership. Joy is built upon partnerships, and that is the kind of ministry Paul has: a relational ministry. He walks with the Corinthians, he suffers with them, he encourages them, he strengthens them, and the result is joy that comes from standing together in the faith.

Being present with one another is so essential for the growth of the Christian. During COVID we found that streaming is not the same as worshiping God together. We cannot really worship God corporately via the internet. There is a presence that builds joy.

### The Bike-Riding Illustration

Have you ever taught anybody to ride a bike? Stephen and Adam, can you help me out with the object lesson if you do not mind? That is what happens when you sit up front.

Okay, Adam, you are learning to ride a bike. Steve, you are teaching Adam how to ride a bike. How does that look?

[After the demonstration] I do counseling on Thursdays if you guys ever need to talk about this. That did not work — I will just use my words to illustrate the point.

Often when a father is teaching his kids to ride a bike, he runs alongside them, cheering them, letting the child balance themselves, but coming in to catch them so they do not hurt themselves. Eventually the child rides confidently, laughing with great joy. That is this idea of coming alongside — protecting from hurt, and yet eventually the person just going with great joy. That is what we are looking for.

If we are lording over people, that is not going to happen. They are not going to learn to ride the bike for themselves. There will be fear and guilt — but no bike riding. But if we refuse to lord over people and instead serve them for their joy, then everybody is going to grow in their faith.

## III. Standing Firm in Faith Through Partnership

Look again at verse 24:

> *"Not that we have dominion over your faith, but are fellow workers for your joy; for by faith you stand."* (2 Corinthians 1:24)

Faith is strengthened by partnership. I hope you have somebody in your life that you look forward to being with — does not have to be your spouse, but somebody in your life you look forward to being with all the time because being with them feels like being in the presence of God. Where you leave as if you are like Moses who came down the mountain, shining, reflecting the glory of God. Somebody whose partnership makes your faith strengthen and grow.

Paul is not making the Corinthians dependent on himself. He is strengthening their ability to stand. You remember the passage in Ephesians 6 about the armor of God? Read the introduction to that passage and count how many times the word "stand" is used. How do we stand by faith?

In 1 Corinthians 15:58, Paul says:

> *"Therefore, my beloved brethren, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that your labor is not in vain in the Lord."* (1 Corinthians 15:58)

Why can Paul encourage the Corinthians to that? Because he served them for their joy, and now they can stand in their faith.

The author of Hebrews tells us to do the same in Hebrews 10:23-24:

> *"Let us hold fast the confession of our hope without wavering, for He who promised is faithful. And let us consider one another in order to stir up love and good works."* (Hebrews 10:23-24)

This idea of "stir up" is a word used in Greek literature to describe the relationship between an older brother and a younger brother, where the younger brother is always pushing and poking the older brother. But in the Bible, we are not to stir each other up into trouble — we are to stir up love, so that out of that poking, good works come.

Standing firm does not mean standing alone. It means standing with encouragement and guidance from faithful partners.

## IV. Applications

Let me give four applications for four different groups.

**For church leaders** — elders, deacons, brothers: we need to lead humbly. We need to build faith and joy in others, not be bosses or masters. We need to avoid the temptation to want the sheep to serve us when we are called to serve the sheep.

**For all believers** — brothers and sisters: partner with others. Walk alongside others. Encourage people. Be mentored by people. Share the gospel without controlling others. Walk together. We often talk about the Amos passage on marriage: "Can two walk together unless they are agreed?" Well, we need to be walking together because we are agreed — and we are agreed in Jesus Christ. We need each other. Sometimes the walk will look more like somebody carrying us; sometimes we are doing the carrying. We need to be walking together for each other's joy, that we might grow in our faith.

**For families** — kids, look up, stop drawing, look up. Sometimes it feels like it is only mom and dad's job to do the spiritual things in the house. But it is also your job as brothers and sisters — as siblings — to encourage each other in Christ. Malachi is not only supposed to try to get into trouble; he is also supposed to encourage Miles in the Lord. Miles is not supposed to just be the one that people keep telling what to do; he is supposed to come alongside Ashlyn and encourage Ashlyn in the Lord. Maria is to encourage Kali. And I guess Abigail should encourage Madeline, and in the future Liz is to encourage Aubrey — and you get the idea. That is not just true of relationships out there, but in the family as well. You kids are each other's best friends. Point each other to the Lord. Make sure you all cross into heaven together.

**In daily life** — encourage and partner with each other at work and at school. We are called to work together, not by ourselves.

God's work is done together, not alone. I think the best way to do missions is not to send one guy or even one family, but to send a pastor family, a deacon family, and an elder family all to go plant a church in a place where Christ has not been before — because ministry is done together. Ministry is partnership, not domination. True growth produces joy, produces resilience, produces confidence in the faith.

So ask yourself: Am I trying to control others, or am I working with them for their joy? Am I encouraging and building faith, or demanding obedience? Growing together produces better results than growing alone. Ministry, family, and church flourish through partnership.

And ultimately, the thing to take away from this message is: never ask Steve or Adam to teach kids to ride a bike.
